file_verdicts.summary: Standard AUR binary package from official upstream; no malicious code.

audits.body:

Analyzing unbill-cli-nightly-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-n0fc1qyn) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard binary package for the nightly release of unbill-cli. The global scope contains only variable definitions (pkgver, pkgdesc, source, etc.) and no command substitutions or dangerous operations. The `source` array points to the official GitHub release URL of the project's own repository, which is expected and legitimate. There is no `pkgver()` function that could execute code during sourcing. The `package()` function is not executed by the commands in scope. No malicious exfiltration, code execution, or unexpected network destinations are present. The use of `SKIP` checksum is acceptable for a binary release from GitHub, and while not ideal for reproducibility, it is not a security threat.</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with no malicious code in global scope.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard PKGBUILD with no malicious code in global scope.[/]

makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD downloads a pre-built binary from the project's official GitHub releases page (`github.com/unbill-project/unbill`), which is the expected upstream source. The `sha256sums` are set to `'SKIP'`; while this is a reproducibility concern for a non-VCS source, it is a common practice in AUR binary packages when the upstream does not provide checksums. There are no obfuscated commands, suspicious network destinations, unexpected file operations, or attempts to execute untrusted code beyond the standard `install` command to place the binary into `$pkgdir/usr/bin/`. The package does nothing that deviates from routine packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary package from official upstream; no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

file_verdicts.summary: Standard nightly binary package with expected practices

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/unbill-cli-nightly-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bfud9tgw...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bfud9tgw
Analyzing unbill-cli-nightly-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bfud9tgw) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array that fetches a binary from the project's official GitHub releases page using a predictable tag format. The source URL interpolation uses a `_tag` variable set within the PKGBUILD, which is not dynamically fetched or obfuscated. The `sha256sums_x86_64` is set to 'SKIP', which is acceptable for nightly binary packages where reproducibility is not guaranteed, and is not inherently malicious.

Sourcing this PKGBUILD during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `--nobuild` execution would only evaluate the global variables and possibly call `pkgver()` (if defined), but no such function is present. There are no command substitutions, function calls, or external executions at the top level. The `source` array uses a direct HTTPS link to a known upstream, and no code is downloaded or executed during sourcing. The package function, which contains file installation logic, is not executed under the specified flags.

As such, merely sourcing this PKGBUILD and downloading the listed source is safe. The binary will be placed in the srcdir without any processing at this stage. There is no evidence of malicious payload injection, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network activity during this phase.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: no executable code during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: no executable code during source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a straightforward binary package that fetches a pre-built `unbill-cli` executable from the project's official GitHub releases. The source URL uses an interpolated `_tag` variable matching the release tag format of the upstream repository (`https://github.com/unbill-project/unbill`), which is consistent with standard AUR practices for `-bin` packages. The `sha256sums_x86_64=('SKIP')` is expected for nightly packages where the binary changes frequently, though it reduces reproducibility; however, this does not constitute a security threat by itself.

The package() function safely installs the downloaded binary with appropriate permissions to `/usr/bin/unbill-cli`. There are no signs of malicious behavior such as obfuscated code, unauthorized network requests, or file system modifications beyond the package directory. All dependencies, conflicts, and provides are logically related to the package itself, and no scripts or hooks perform unexpected operations.

While skipping checksum verification is a hygiene concern for supply-chain integrity, it does not cross into maliciousness—especially in a `-nightly` context. No evidence of data exfiltration, runtime injection, or execution of fetched code beyond the declared source is present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard nightly binary package with expected practices
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...

No issues found.

data.reasoning: This is a simple binary package that downloads a pre-built executable from the project's official GitHub releases (github.com/unbill-project/unbill) and installs it to /usr/bin via the package() function. Both models reviewed the PKGBUILD and reached the same conclusion. The package has only a single file to review (PKGBUILD); makepkg --printsrcinfo and source download succeeded, and no .install hooks or auxiliary scripts were present. There is no build/prepare/check logic, no command substitution at global scope, and no pkgver() function. The only hygiene note is sha256sums set to 'SKIP', which is normal and expected for a -nightly -bin package and not a security threat.
learnings: The 'shallow' trigger (1 file reviewed) is a false alarm here: the package genuinely contains only a PKGBUILD with no .install hooks or auxiliary scripts, so 1 file is complete coverage. The tool should distinguish 'reviewed 1 of N files' from 'package only has 1 reviewable file' before flagging shallow reviews.

For -bin/-nightly packages, SKIP checksums are routine and correctly identified by both models as a non-malicious hygiene concern; the prompts handled this well and should continue treating it as informational rather than a finding.

Both models correctly noted the binary is unverifiable but did not overreach into calling it unsafe — good calibration. However, neither flagged that the actual downloaded binary's behavior cannot be assessed from the PKGBUILD alone; for -bin packages this is an inherent and acceptable trust limitation, but the tool could surface it explicitly to the user as 'binary contents not auditable from source'.

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD as safe: downloads binary from official GitHub repo, no malicious operations in global scope or package() function. SKIP checksum is acceptable for nightly binaries.
coverage_issues: Both models only reviewed PKGBUILD. No .install hooks or other scripts present in package, so no critical gaps.
false_negatives: Models didn't flag SKIP checksum as supply-chain risk (though acceptable for nightly). Low severity.
learnings: Improve file prioritization: Models should explicitly confirm absence of hooks/scripts
Enhance checksum risk assessment: Clarify when SKIP is acceptable vs. risky
Add coverage notes: Reports should explicitly state why other files (e.g., .install) weren't reviewed
